title: The Greenspan Lesson for the AI Race
gist: CSIS senior fellow Karl Smith argues the Federal Reserve risks repeating the mistake of over-tightening by relying on backward-looking statistics that miss a nascent AI productivity surge, directly analogous to the Greenspan Fed's 1990s gamble. With Silicon Valley's self-financed AI buildout set to end in 2026, borrowing costs will become a binding constraint on U.S. AI capital investment. Allowing rates to stay too high would cede long-term technological and strategic advantage to China.

themes.theme: Fed Policy as AI Geopolitical Risk

insights.claim: The Federal Reserve's reliance on backward-looking inflation and unemployment statistics risks misidentifying the current AI productivity surge, potentially leading to rate decisions that stifle U.S. technological competitiveness against China.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Overly restrictive Fed policy is not merely an economic risk but a national security risk; investors should weight the probability that geopolitical pressure becomes a new political constraint on Fed tightening, creating a dovish policy floor.
insights.quote: U.S. macroeconomic institutions could misread a new productivity paradigm. As a result, they could raise interest, slow AI investment, and weaken the country's long-term technological position.

insights.claim: AI capital investment will require trillions of dollars in external financing and the era of Silicon Valley self-funding is likely ending in 2026, making the Federal Reserve's rate stance a direct determinant of the pace of AI infrastructure buildout.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Rate cuts or a credible dovish pivot directly expand the total addressable financing for data centers, power infrastructure, and satellite launches; duration-sensitive infrastructure plays benefit disproportionately.
insights.quote: Until now, Silicon Valley has been able to mostly self-finance the AI buildout. That era will likely come to an end sometime this year. The cost of that financing will depend largely on where the Federal Reserve sets interest rates.
insights.locator: section: Looking Out the Window in Today's Economy

themes.theme: AI Productivity Hidden in Statistics — Greenspan Parallel

insights.claim: AI capabilities are already ubiquitous in software development and spreading across the economy, but this productivity transformation is not yet captured in official U.S. statistics, mirroring exactly the Solow computer paradox of the early 1990s.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Weak official productivity prints should not be taken at face value as evidence against an AI productivity boom; the statistical lag creates a temporary window where the market may underprice productivity-leveraged equities.
insights.quote: Just outside the window is an economy where AI is everywhere but in the productivity statistics. The entire software development industry, home to some of the world's most dynamic companies, has undergone a radical change overnight.
insights.locator: section: Looking Out the Window in Today's Economy

insights.claim: The cost of a given level of AI performance has fallen by 9x to 900x per year depending on the benchmark, a deflationary dynamic that official price indices almost certainly do not capture.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Unmeasured AI price deflation systematically overstates real-economy inflation, providing a hidden argument for the Fed to tolerate above-target CPI readings; this supports the case for longer bond duration if the thesis is correct.
insights.quote: The cost of AI capabilities is dropping at a rate too rapid to measure. The best analysis suggests that, depending on the benchmark, the price of a given level of AI performance has fallen by ninefold to nine-hundredfold per year.
insights.locator: section: Looking Out the Window in Today's Economy

insights.claim: Greenspan's decision to hold and cut rates through 1992-1993 despite models suggesting near-full employment enabled a productivity boom that lifted U.S. GDP 14% above its counterfactual baseline by 2003, with unemployment eventually bottoming at 3.9% without triggering the feared inflation.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The 1990s precedent quantifies the stakes: tolerating statistical ambiguity during a productivity transition delivered multi-percentage-point cumulative GDP gains; the AI analog implies similar or larger macro upside if policy does not preemptively tighten.
insights.quote: All in all, based on these numbers, the U.S. economy was 14 percent larger by 2003 than it would have been without the productivity surge.
insights.locator: section: The Boom That Became Strategic Infrastructure

themes.theme: Dot-Com Investment as Strategic Infrastructure — Historical Vindication

insights.claim: Despite the dot-com crash and the 9/11 recession, U.S. labor productivity continued to grow above 2% through 2003, averaging 3.15% per year from 1996 to 2003, validating the underlying productive value of the 1990s capital binge even after speculative excess was purged.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Even if an AI bubble forms and corrects, the underlying infrastructure investment likely generates durable productivity gains; investors should distinguish between valuation risk (high) and productive capital destruction risk (lower) in AI infrastructure assets.
insights.quote: Despite both the dot-com crash and the recession following 9/11, labor productivity continued to grow above 2 percent through 2003. The average yearly increase from 1996 to 2003 was 3.15 percent.
insights.locator: section: The Boom That Became Strategic Infrastructure

insights.claim: The Magnificent Seven — Alphabet, Apple, Meta, Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft, Tesla — account for one-third of S&P 500 market cap and their combined market cap exceeds that of the EU and Japan, a direct legacy of the 1990s investment boom enabled by Greenspan's dovish policy stance.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The compounding of 1990s infrastructure investment into today's Mag-7 dominance illustrates the extreme long-run returns to being the capital formation winner in a productivity transition; the AI cycle's analogous winners may similarly dominate indices for decades.
insights.quote: The Magnificent Seven stocks—Alphabet (Google), Apple, Meta, Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft, and Tesla—account for a third of the S&P 500's market capitalization. Those seven companies alone exceed the combined market capitalization of the European Union and Japan.
insights.locator: section: The Boom That Became Strategic Infrastructure

themes.theme: AI Infrastructure Capital Cycle — Breadth and Scale

insights.claim: Winning the AI race against China will require U.S. corporations to borrow trillions to finance data centers, power generation, electricity transmission, autonomous vehicle factories, and dozens of communication and computation satellites per day — a capital program of unprecedented peacetime scale.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The scope of required investment points to sustained multi-year tailwinds across data center REITs, utilities, grid infrastructure, satellite launch providers, and semiconductor capital equipment; this is not a single-sector story.
insights.quote: It will require data centers, power generation, electricity transmission, autonomous vehicle factories, and dozens of communication and computation satellites launched into low Earth orbit every single day.

insights.claim: The existing Magnificent Seven companies — particularly Microsoft (cloud/OpenAI), Amazon (cloud), Google (AI research/transformers), Nvidia (chips), Meta (open source models), and Apple (device distribution) — constitute the pre-built operating system for U.S. AI dominance, giving them structural moats in the current AI race.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The incumbency advantage of Mag-7 in AI is structural, not merely financial; new AI labs like OpenAI and Anthropic are themselves dependent on this infrastructure stack, limiting the threat of pure-play AI disintermediation of the incumbents.
insights.quote: Microsoft and Amazon built the cloud platforms on which the AI economy runs. Google built the research culture that produced the transformer and remains one of the world's leading AI labs. Nvidia supplied the chips that turned deep learning from a research program into an industrial race.
insights.locator: section: The Boom That Became Strategic Infrastructure

insights.claim: Stubbornly above-target U.S. inflation may be partially an artifact of measurement failure rather than genuine demand-side overheating, because official statistics cannot adequately capture the deflationary effect of rapidly falling AI capability costs spreading across the economy.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: If mismeasurement is a significant contributor to above-2% CPI, the Fed is tighter than it realizes in real terms; this is a bull case for risk assets and longer duration Treasuries that is not yet priced into consensus.
insights.locator: section: Looking Out the Window in Today's Economy

insights.claim: In 1992, consumer confidence was at historic lows with 90% of Americans calling the economy fair or poor, yet this pessimistic sentiment coincided with the beginning of the longest peacetime expansion in U.S. history — a warning against conflating consumer sentiment with actual productive capacity.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Current consumer or business pessimism about the economy should not be treated as a leading indicator of productive slowdown; sentiment-driven positioning may overprice recession risk during genuine productivity transitions.
insights.quote: Only 10 percent of Americans described the economy as good or excellent, compared with 90 percent who called it fair or poor. Those were the worst numbers ever recorded outside of a recession.
